title: How to Tell Your Inner Voice from Anxiety
subtitle: A reading on the practical difference between intuition and fear, and why it matters
excerpt: A reading on distinguishing intuition from anxiety. Looks at how the two feel different in the body and the thoughts they produce, why anxious thought patterns mimic intuition, and what practical indicators can help separate a real signal from a worried one.
sourceNotes: Written for homemade.education. Draws on somatic and psychological accounts of the difference between intuition and anxiety, including Antonio Damasio, Descartes' Error (1994), on somatic markers in decision-making, and clinical accounts of the overlap between intuition and anxiety in people with high trait anxiety.

content.text: A reading on how to tell intuition from anxiety. The two often feel similar from inside. That makes the difference matter. Acting on anxiety as if it were intuition leads to poor decisions. Dismissing intuition as anxiety means missing real signals.
The problem with trusting your gut

Advice to 'trust your gut' assumes that the gut is producing a reliable signal. It often is. But anxious thought patterns also produce physical sensations, persistent concerns, and strong urges to act. An anxious person can experience the physical pull to leave a relationship, change a job, or avoid a situation with the same intensity as genuine intuition. The sensation alone is not sufficient evidence.
This is not a reason to distrust intuition. It is a reason to distinguish it from anxiety rather than treating them as the same thing.

Intuition tends to feel quiet, not loud. It is steady, not jumpy. It does not need to be justified again and again. It points toward something, rather than away from everything. When it is ignored, it does not turn into catastrophe thinking. It simply remains.
Intuition often shows up as a low-level, persistent sense that something is right or wrong, rather than an urgent alarm. It does not require immediate action. It can be tested.

Anxiety tends to feel loud and pushy. It rises and falls, often worse at night or under stress. It throws up worst-case stories rather than clear concerns. Reassurance does not settle it. It tends to spread across many situations rather than land on one.

Anxious thought also tends to produce the same content repeatedly. The loop of a worried mind is not the calm return of an intuitive signal. It is the same thought recycled. If the concern is generating new information each time you return to it, that is more likely to be productive reflection. If it is replaying the same fear, that is more likely to be anxiety.

One practical test: put the concern aside deliberately for twenty-four hours. Anxiety generally gets louder or finds a new target. Intuition generally waits and returns to the same place. A second test: ask what specific action the signal is pointing to. Intuition tends to produce a specific direction. Anxiety tends to produce an imperative to escape without a clear destination.
Neither test is definitive. Both are more useful than intensity alone, which is the measure most people default to.

People with high anxiety often have good intuitions that they have learned not to trust because they can't tell them apart from the anxious noise. The work is not to suppress intuition but to learn the difference between the two signals. That takes practice and pays back over time.

Written for homemade.education. The reading draws on body-based research into gut feelings and decision-making. It also draws on accounts of how intuition and anxiety overlap in people who run anxious.
